coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots as an effective fluorescence probe for the detection of doxorubicin and cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wenkun
Ma, Rong
Gu, Shuo
Zhang, Liting
Li, Ningbo
Qiao, Jie
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (NPCDs) with bright blue fluorescence were established by a hydrothermal method using alanine and diammonium phosphate as raw materials. The NPCDs were characterized by transmitted electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectrometry, ultraviolet–visible absorption (UV–vis), and fluorescence spectroscopy to investigate the morphology, elemental composition, and optical properties. The NPCDs had good water solubility, high dispersibility with an average diameter of only 2.77 nm, and satisfactory optical properties with a fluorescence quantum yield of 17.2%. The NPCDs were employed for the detection of doxorubicin (DOX). A good linear response of the NPCDs in the range 0.5–6.5 μM was obtained for DOX with a detection limit of 12 nM. The NPCDs were also applied to the analysis of real samples, urine and serum, with a recovery of 92.0–109.3%. The low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility of the NPCDs were indicated by an MTT assay and cell imaging of HeLa cells. Compared with other detection systems, using NPCDs for DOX detection was a facile and efficient method with good selectivity and sensitivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N and P co-Doped CDs
Fluorescent probe
Doxorubicin
Static quenching
Biocompatibility

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of DOX and interference experiments
Detection of DOX in biological samples
Detection of DOX and selectivity of the method
Determination of DOX in biological samples
Cytotoxicity of NPCDs and its application in cell imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Effect of reaction temperature; (b) Effect of reaction time; (c) Effect of the molar ratio of raw materials.
(a) TEM images of NPCDs (Inset: size distribution of NPCDs); (b) HRTEM image of NPCDs.
(a) XPS full spectrum of NPCDs; (b) C1s; (c) N1s; (d) O1s; (e) P2p fine spectra and (f) FT-IR spectrum of NPCDs.
(a) UV–vis absorption and fluorescent excitation and emission spectra of the NPCDs; (b) fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths.

(a) The fluorescence intensity of the NPCDs and NPCDs-DOX system at different pH; (b) Effect of the fluorescence intensity of NPCDs-DOX system at 3.2–4.0 mg/mL; (c) Effect of the reaction time on the fluorescence intensity of NPCDs-DOX system.

(a) The fluorescence quenching of the NPCDs at various concentrations of DOX; (b) Stern-Volmer linear plot of F0/F versus the concentration of DOX; and (c, d) effect of common metal ions and biomolecules on the NPCDs.

The UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of the NPCDs; (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of the NPCDs solution after addition of various concentrations of DOX; (c) fluorescence lifetime in the presence of NPCDs and NPCDs-DOX system; (d) Stern-Volmer plots of NPCDs-DOX system at different temperatures.
Cells viability of at different NPCDs concentrations.

The confocal fluorescence images of HeLa cells incubated with NPCDs in bright field (A) and blue channel (B); and confocal fluorescence images of HeLa cells incubated with NPCDs and DOX in bright field (C) and blue channels (D).
Synthesis of NPCDs and detection of DOX.
Comparison of various methods for DOX detection.
Determination of DOX in human urine and serum samples.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots as an effective fluorescence probe for the detection of doxorubicin and cell imaging

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (NPCDs) with bright blue fluorescence were established by a hydrothermal method using alanine and diammonium phosphate as raw materials. The NPCDs were characterized by transmitted electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectrometry, ultraviolet–visible absorption (UV–vis), and fluorescence spectroscopy to investigate the morphology, elemental composition, and optical properties. The NPCDs had good water solubility, high dispersibility with an average diameter of only 2.77 nm, and satisfactory optical properties with a fluorescence quantum yield of 17.2%. The NPCDs were employed for the detection of doxorubicin (DOX). A good linear response of the NPCDs in the range 0.5–6.5 μM was obtained for DOX with a detection limit of 12 nM. The NPCDs were also applied to the analysis of real samples, urine and serum, with a recovery of 92.0–109.3%. The low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility of the NPCDs were indicated by an MTT assay and cell imaging of HeLa cells. Compared with other detection systems, using NPCDs for DOX detection was a facile and efficient method with good selectivity and sensitivity.

Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (NPCDs) were constructed by a hydrothermal method using alanine and diammonium phosphate as raw materials, and can be used for the detection of doxorubicin with good selectivity and sensitivity.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Doxorubicin (DOX) is an important anthraquinone-based antibiotic, which is widely used for the treatment of many kinds of cancer such as lung cancer, breast cancer, and other tumor cells [1,2]. Although DOX can be used as an effective anti-cancer drug to kill tumor cells, it can also cause damage to cardiotoxicity, liver damage, hair loss, and bone marrow suppression [3]. Thus, it is necessary to monitor DOX concentration in real-time for effective treatment and minimizing the harm to human health. To date, many chemical techniques have been proposed to determine the DOX content, including high-performance liquid chromatography [4], capillary electrophoresis [5], chemiluminescence [6], mass spectrometry [7], and electrochemical methods [8]. However, the above methods suffer from certain drawbacks, such as the high cost and time-consuming pre-processing requirements, poor sensitivity and selectivity, and long response times. Therefore, a method for the simple, accurate, and inexpensive determination of DOX is becoming increasingly important in medicine and pharmacology.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new member of the carbon nanomaterial family and have received much attention because of their cost-effective preparation, large specific surface area, optical properties, low toxicity, and biocompatibility [9–11]. CDs have been widely used in bioimaging [12], biosensors [13], photoelectric catalysis [14], nanomedicines [15], drug transport [16], and environmental detection [17]. In particular, heteroatom doping of CDs can increase quantum yields and provide CDs with more stable optical properties [18,19]. Thus, Fluorescence analysis using doped carbon dots is a promising platform for chemical, biological, and environmental detection [20]. For example, Song et al. have reported an effective fluorescence probe for the detection of DOX using sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (S, N-CDs) [21]. Recently, Wei et al. synthesized plum-based carbon quantum dots (PCQDs) via a facile bottom-up method, which were used to determine DOX [22]. However, these synthetic doped carbon dots for DOX detection showed a moderate quantum yield and a high detection limit. Hence, it is an urgent need to construct doped carbon dots as a highly efficient and selective fluorescence probe for the detection of DOX.

It is known that the doping of nitrogen into CDs has been proved to provide potential active sites and increase the quantum yield [23]. However, there are some drawbacks of N-doped CDs such as poor dispersion in water and ease of interference by metal ions, acid, or base [24,25]. To overcome these defects, phosphorus atom was often used for doping the N-doped CDs to generate nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots, which can improve the water dispersion and the quantum yield. In recent years, N and P co-doped CDs can be used as an effective fluorescence probe for metal ion detection such as Cr2+, Fe3+, Mn2+ [26–28]. However, there have been no reports of NPCDs being used to detect DOX. Based on the great research prospects of NPCDs, we envisage the synthesis of NPCDs as a fluorescence probe for the detection of DOX.

In this work, nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (NPCDs) with a high quantum yield of 17.2% were successfully synthesized from alanine and diammonium phosphate by a hydrothermal method and characterized by different techniques. The NPCDs showed good sensitivity and selectivity for DOX detection with a detection limit of 12 nM. The NPCDs could also be used for the detection of DOX in urine and serum. In addition, the NPCDs showed low toxicity and good biocompatibility, which was determined by an MTT assay and the cell imaging of HeLa cells (Scheme 1 ).

Alanine, diammonium phosphate, doxorubicin hydrochloride, and other related reagents were analytically pure and purchased from Aladdin. Deionized water was used in the experiment. The morphology was determined on a transmission electron microscope (JEOL JEM-2100F, Japan). Bruker Tensor II Fourier infrared spectrometer (Bremen, Germany) was used to analyze the functional group. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out with a Kratos AXIS ULTRA DLD spectrometer. The absorption spectrum was recorded using a UV-2910 ultraviolet spectrophotometer. Fluorescence experiments were measured with a Hitachi F-4500 fluorescent spectrophotometer. Digital pH meter SJ-3F (Shanghai Leigu, China) was used to adjust the pH of the PBS. The fluorescent images of HeLa cells were performed on an FV1000-MPE multiphoton laser scanning microscope (Olympus, Japan). The dry samples were obtained in an FD-1D-50 freeze vacuum dryer (Beijing Boyikang, China).

For the preparation of the NPCDs, 0.2070 g of alanine and 0.1021 g of diammonium phosphate were mixed in a 50-mL flask and dissolved in 10.0 mL of water with violent stirring. The homogeneous mixture was then transferred into a 50-mL autoclave and kept at 200 °C for 8 h in an oven. After the autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature, the NPCDs solution was filtered with a 0.22-μm microporous membrane. Then, dialysis (molecular weight cut-off of 1000 D) against distilled water for 48 h was used to remove any residual species. Finally, solid residues were obtained by freeze-drying the purified yellow solution under a vacuum.

The fluorescent quantum yield of the NPCDs solution was measured by reference to a quinine sulfate solution (0.1 M) using an ultraviolet spectrophotometer to ensure that the absorbance value of the quinine sulfate and the NPCDs solution at 360 nm was <0.05. The quantum yields of the NPCDs and quinine sulfate were calculated using the equation (QY
R = 0.54, η
R = 1.33).
Q Y = Q
Y R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
η 2
η R
2
where QY and QY
R represent the fluorescent quantum yields of the sample and the reference solution, respectively; I and I
R represent the integral areas of the fluorescence spectrum of the sample and the reference solution, respectively; A and A
R are the UV–visible absorbent values of the sample and the reference solution, respectively; and η and η
R indicate the refractive index of the sample and the reference solution, respectively (the refractive index of the sulfuric acid solution and water in this experiment were the same).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DOX and interference experiments
ce:section.ce:para: For the fluorometric detection of DOX, 20 μL of NPCDs solution (3.8 mg/mL) was poured into a 10-mm quartz cell, then different volumes of DOX solution (10−4 mol/L) were added and the final volume of the mixture was made up to 2.0 mL with PBS (10 mM, pH = 7.0). The above solution was gently shaken and reacted for 10 min at room temperature. The fluorescence spectrum was measured at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm by setting the slit width to 5 nm. The responses were monitored from 380 to 700 nm. For selectivity experiments, the common metal ions (Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, and K+) and biomolecules (Glc, Fru, GSH, DA, VC, Cre, UA, Trp, His, Gly, Ile, Met, Glu, Lys, Phe, Ala, Thr, Pro, Cys, Tyr, lactose, sucrose, urea, and thiourea) replaced the DOX in the system and the experiments were performed using the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DOX in biological samples
ce:section.ce:para: Serum samples were collected from clinical laboratories, and urine samples were collected from a healthy volunteer in our laboratory, neither type of sample contained any DOX. Both types of samples were first filtered with a 0.22-μm membrane, then centrifuged for 30 min (8000 r/min). Then, the supernatant was separated to fabricate spiked samples and stored at 4 °C. The supernatant was diluted 100 times with PBS buffer solution (10 mmol/L, pH = 7.0) before use.

HeLa cells were incubated under standard conditions (37 °C and 5% CO2). When the cells covered the wall of the culture flask, the cells were digested and shifted to a 96-well plate. The cell density of each well reached 5 × 103 cells/mL and the number of cells was recorded by a cell count plate. Different concentrations of NPCDs (0, 5, 10, 70, 80, 90, 700, 800, 900, and 1000 μg/mL) were introduced into the cells and the cells were incubated for 24 h. Then, 20 μL of MTT (0.5 mg/mL) was added to the cells and the cells were incubated for 4 h. Finally, the MTT was replaced with 150 μL of DMSO in each well. After mixing for 15 min at room temperature, the absorbance was determined using a microplate reader.

HeLa cells were suspended in a 35-mm Petri dish with 10-mm holes at the bottom and cultured for 24 h. After washing with PBS buffer, the HeLa cells were incubated with 3.8 mg/mL NPCDs at 37 °C for 1.5 h (control group). In the experimental group, 10 μM DOX was added to the cells and the cells were incubated for 1.5 h. After removing the supernatant, the cells were washed with PBS and then fixed with 4% p-formaldehyde for 10 min. Finally, the fluorescence images were recorded using a confocal laser scanning microscope.

The NPCDs were prepared using alanine and diammonium phosphate as raw materials by a hydrothermal method. The reaction temperature, reaction time, and the molar ratios of the raw materials were optimized in terms of the fluorescence properties (Fig. 1 ). The maximum fluorescence emission for the NPCDs was obtained by the reaction of a 2:1 M ratio of alanine and diammonium phosphate at 200 °C for 8 h measured at 441 nm.

The prepared NPCDs were well dispersed in water and had bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light at 365 nm. The TEM images revealed that the NPCDs were spherical with an average particle size of 2.77 nm [Fig. 2 a (inset)], which indicated a good dispersion. Fig. 2b shows the high-resolution TEM diagram of NPCDs at a scale of 5 nm. The lattice spacing of NPCDs is 0.22 nm, indicating that NPCDs have a graphite-like structure [29].

The elemental compositions of the NPCDs determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) are shown in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3a shows the absorption peaks at 530.6 eV, 400.0 eV, 284.8 eV, 188 eV, and 134.6 eV, corresponding to the binding energy of O1s, N1s, C1s, P2s, and P2p, indicating that the NPCDs contain C, N, O, and P atoms [30]. The C1s spectrum in Fig. 3b shows peaks at 284.7, 285.8, 287.6, and 288.6 eV, which indicates the presence of CC, C–N, C–O, and CO groups, respectively [31]. As can be seen from Fig. 3c, the N1s spectrum was consistent with C–N (400.0 eV) and N–H (401.2 eV) [32]. The O1s spectra in Fig. 3d indicated the presence of CO (531.1 eV) and C–OH/C–O–C (532.6 eV) [33]. The P 2p spectrum in Fig. 3e shows peaks at 133.5 and 134.2 eV, which indicates the presence of P–C, and P–C/P–N [34]. The FT-IR spectrum shown in Fig. 3f shows the surface functional groups of the NPCDs. The wide absorption peak at 3414 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration peak of the O–H/N–H bond [35]. The stretching vibration peak at 3107 cm−1 is attributed to the C–H bond [36]. The stretching vibration peak of the CO bond appears at 1623 cm−1 and the in-plane bending vibration peaks of C–H and C–N appear at 1390 and 1406 cm−1, respectively [37,38]. The absorption peaks at 1260 and 1675 cm−1 may be the stretching vibrations of the PO and P–O-R bond, respectively [39].

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescent excitation/emission spectra of the NPCDs are shown in Fig. 4 . The maximum UV–vis absorption peak of the NPCDs was at approximately 280 nm, which was caused by the n-π* transition of the CO group [40]. When the excitation wavelength was 360 nm, the maximum emission wavelength was 441 nm (Fig. 4a). The solution of the NPCDs appeared light yellow under a daylight lamp and emitted bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light (inset, λex = 365 nm), which was consistent with the emission spectrum of the NPCDs. In addition, we investigated the excitation-dependent behavior at different excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 4b, when the excitation wavelength changed from 310 to 360 nm, the fluorescence intensity increased continuously; however, the fluorescence intensity decreased with an increase in excitation wavelengths from 360 to 420 nm. This result indicated that the emission wavelength depended on the excitation wavelength. The blueshift of the peak position may be related to the particle sizes and different emission sites on the surface of the NPCDs [41]. The maximum emission peak (441 nm) was observed when the excitation wavelength was 360 nm. An excitation wavelength of 360 nm was selected for subsequent experiments. The fluorescence quantum yield of the NPCDs was calculated to be 17.2%, using quinine sulfate as a standard, which was higher than the reported fluorescence quantum yield from natural sources [22].

Fig. 5 a shows the effect of the pH (4.0–11.0) of the PBS solution on the fluorescence intensities of the NPCDs and NPCDs-DOX systems. F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities of the NPCDs and NPCDs-DOX, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of the NPCDs showed little change with an increase in the pH in the absence of DOX. However, the fluorescence intensity decreased with the addition of DOX. The value of F0/F increased to maxima at pH 7.0, illustrating the high sensitivity. Thus, a PBS buffer solution with pH 7.0 was selected for use in the subsequent experiments.

Fig. 5b displays the effect of different concentrations of NPCDs-DOX systems using Stern-Volmer linear plots. The slopes of the Stern-Volmer curves increase until the concentration reaches 3.8 mg/mL, and then decrease with a further increase in NPCDs concentration. The possible reason is that high concentration leads to self-absorption which decreases the fluorescence intensity of NPCDs. Thus, a concentration of 3.8 mg/mL NPCDs was selected for subsequent experiments.

The optimal reaction times for the detection of DOX were investigated. The fluorescence intensity was monitored by adding a known concentration of DOX to the NPCDs solution. Fig. 5c shows that with the addition of DOX the fluorescence intensity initially decreased and was almost unchanged after 10 min. Therefore, 10 min was selected as the optimal reaction time for the detection of DOX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DOX and selectivity of the method

ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the quenching mechanism, we used UV–visible absorption and fluorescence lifetime to distinguish static from dynamic quenching. Fig. 7 a shows the UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of the NPCDs, which have an overlap between them. One can see that the UV–vis absorption of DOX produces absorption for the fluorescence excitation of NPCDs, illustrating that there is an internal filter effect between DOX and NPCDs. Fig. 7b shows that the UV–vis absorption intensity of NPCDs was enhanced with increased DOX concentrations, but the absorption wavelength (330 nm) did not shift. This result indicated that the fluorescence quenching of the NPCDs was probably caused by the strong interaction between the NPCDs and DOX. These results tend to support a static quenching mechanism.

A static quenching mechanism was also indicated by the fluorescence lifetime. If static quenching occurs, the fluorescence lifetime should remain unchanged within the margin of error [48,49]. As shown in Fig. 7c, in the absence of DOX, the average fluorescence lifetime of the NPCDs was 5.09 ns. The average fluorescence lifetime changed little, ranging from 5.04 to 5.30 ns, with the addition of different concentrations of DOX, indicating that there was no significant fluorescence resonance energy transfer in the NPCDs-DOX system. In other words, the fluorescence change of the NPCDs was attributed to electrostatic and hydrogen bonding interactions between the NPCDs and DOX, while the fluorescence quenching was because of the color change. In addition, It can be seen from Fig. 7d that the quenching constant (the Stern-Volmer plot) decreases with the increase of temperature, which proves that the quenching mechanism of the NPCDs-DOX system is static quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DOX in biological samples
ce:section.ce:para: To expand the applicability, the NPCDs were used as a fluorescence probe for biological samples. Different concentrations of DOX were added to diluted human urine and serum samples, and the content of DOX in the samples was determined by the standard addition recovery method. As shown in Table 2 , the recovery rates of DOX were 92.0–109.3%, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) were 2.0%–4.1% (RSD< 5%), which implied that the synthetic NPCDs could be a potential candidate for the determination of low levels of DOX in human urine and serum samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of NPCDs and its application in cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of different concentrations of NPCDs toward HeLa cells was determined by the MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 8 , at NPCDs concentrations up to 800 μg/mL, the cell viability was still higher than 80% after incubation for 24 h, indicating the low toxicity of the NPCDs. Inspired by the biocompatibility and low toxicity of the NPCDs, we investigated the ability of the NPCDs to detect DOX in HeLa cells by fluorescence imaging. Fig. 9 shows the confocal fluorescent images of HeLa cells incubated with the NPCDs or a mixture of NPCDs/DOX in bright field (A, C) and blue channel (B, D). We observed that the cell morphology was normal. After incubation with NPCDs, the HeLa cells showed strong blue (B), indicating that the NPCDs had good biocompatibility and low toxicity. When HeLa cells were incubated with NPCDs (3.8 mg/mL) and DOX (10 μM) at 37 °C for 1.5 h, almost no fluorescence was observed in the blue channel (D), indicating that DOX could completely quench the fluorescence of the NPCDs solution. Thus, the NPCDs can be used as a “turn-off” fluorescence probe for the imaging of DOX in cells.

NPCDs with bright blue fluorescence were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using alanine and diammonium phosphate as raw materials and characterized by different techniques. The NPCDs showed good water solubility, high dispersibility, and optical properties with a fluorescence quantum yield of 17.2%. The NPCDs could be used for the detection of DOX with a detection limit of 12 nM. The quenching mechanism of the NPCDs-DOX system was via static quenching. The NPCDs were used for DOX detection in human urine and serum samples with a recovery of 92.0–109.3%. The low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility of the NPCDs were indicated by MTT assay and cell imaging of HeLa cells. Compared with other detection systems, using NPCDs for DOX detection is a facile and efficient method with good sensitivity and selectivity. Thus, this system is expected to have further biological and environmental applications.

Wenkun Zhang: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Data processing, Investigation. Rong Ma: Data curation, Formal analysis. Shuo Gu: Software, Validation. Liting Zhang: Investigation, Methodology. Ningbo Li: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Jie Qiao: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Research Project Supported by Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2021-077); National students' platform for innovation and entrepreneurship training program (202110114027); the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21802093) and Shanxi Graduate Innovation Program (2019SY235).
